text: During the laparoscopic procedure, the surgeon explores the abdominal cavity and highlights proximal jejunal loops fused together in the left upper quadrant.

During visceral lysis, a gangrenous jejunal loop is visible, located twenty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz. The mesentery of the gangrenous loop appears retracted and thickened, likely due to mesenteric venous microthrombosis. The surgeon uses a GIE stapler to section the proximal jejunum in a portion of vital tissue, twenty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz. The mesentery is sectioned using an ultrasonic dissector and metal clips, with careful dissection to avoid injuring the transverse colon and its vascularization.

The surgeon completes the release of the necrotic intestinal loop, measuring approximately twenty centimeters, and sections the distal part. An antiperistaltic side-to-side intracorporeal anastomosis is performed using a GIA stapler. The enterotomy is closed with a double layer of T-zero absorbable running suture, and another absorbable suture is used to reinforce the proximal jejunal stump. The anastomosis is then covered with the omentum.
